title: Kakuna Field Dossier
subtitle: Expanded lore and historical record
warning: High delayed-risk pupal form. Primary hazard: stress emergence, cluster alarm, and Beedrill preparation.

sections.title: Dark Lore
sections.content:
Kakuna is not harmless. It is simply unfinished. A Weedle tells you danger is present. A Kakuna tells you danger is being prepared.

Clicking means you still have time to leave. Buzzing means the Beedrill are already awake. A grove full of Kakuna is an armed room before the soldiers wake.

Kakuna is the warning stage no one respects until it is too late. A Weedle tells you danger is present. A Kakuna tells you danger is being prepared. Inside the shell, soft segments pull inward, limbs form where none were visible, wing tissues gather like folded knives, venom channels thicken, and future stingers grow in darkness. It waits in numbers. A grove full of Kakuna is an armed room before the soldiers wake. The first sign is absence: birds avoid the tree line, small mammals stop using the usual trails, and every living thing nearby understands the stillness has a deadline. Woodcutters called clusters hanging barracks. Clicking means you still have time to leave; buzzing means the Beedrill are awake. Disturbance can accelerate emergence. Burning a Kakuna hedge can trigger early Beedrill from the strongest individuals, wet-winged and furious. Indoor Kakuna infestations in attics, barns, chapels, and sheds have produced newborn swarms in confined rooms. Kakuna handling is treated like explosive safety: do not shake the branch, heat the cluster, store indoors without containment, cut down a Kakuna tree in warm weather, crack the shell to check progress, or battle-train it repeatedly for amusement. Kakuna was confirmed in 1800 after marked Weedle hardened in a bramble enclosure. The Yellow Wake warehouse disaster of 1801 followed a merchant storing live Kakuna in rafters to repel mice; warm weather brought emergence and trapped workers inside.

Kakuna is not harmless. It is simply unfinished. That is the mistake people make when they see one hanging from a branch, still and golden, tucked among leaves like a seed pod. It does not crawl like Weedle, chase, sting unless handled badly, or visibly threaten. Children knock them from trees with sticks. Trainers carry them for easy battles. Farmers cut them from hedges and toss them into baskets. Then, days later, the forest fills with Beedrill.

A Weedle tells you danger is present. A Kakuna tells you danger is being prepared. Inside the shell, the larval body is rearranging into something sharper. Soft segments pull inward. Limbs form where none were visible. Wing tissues gather like folded knives. Venom channels thicken. Future stingers grow in darkness, pressed tight against the inner casing. The creature cannot flee while this happens, so it waits in numbers.

A single Kakuna on a branch is a curiosity. A grove full of Kakuna is an armed room before the soldiers wake. The first sign of a Kakuna cluster is absence: birds avoid the tree line, small mammals stop using the usual trails, and even careless predators learn to leave those golden shapes alone after the first bitter mistake. The air feels tense because every living thing nearby understands that stillness has a deadline. Old Kanto woodcutters called these clusters hanging barracks.

Kakuna often form in groups after Weedle swarms feed heavily in brambles, orchards, old hedgerows, battlefield shrubs, or abandoned gardens. They anchor vertically with the head angled outward and the lower body protected by leaves, thorns, or neighboring shells. When disturbed, they harden. Some flex slightly, clicking against branches or other Kakuna. A cluster may respond in waves: one click, then ten, then hundreds. Buzzing means the Beedrill are already awake. Clicking means you still have time to leave.

If one Kakuna is cracked, the others may harden in response. If several are damaged, the cluster can enter stress acceleration. Under certain conditions, disturbance can speed emergence. A farmer who burns a Kakuna hedge may not kill the cluster. Smoke, heat, and damage may trigger early Beedrill emergence from the strongest individuals. They come out malformed, furious, wet-winged, and unable to fly well at first, but they can still sting. A fully healthy Kakuna waits. A damaged Kakuna hurries. A hurried Beedrill does not know restraint.

Kakuna infestations inside buildings are especially feared. They prefer sheltered vertical surfaces: attic beams, barn rafters, orchard ladders, chapel eaves, bridge supports, and abandoned sheds. A building with Kakuna in the beams may appear empty for days. The only signs are faint tapping at night, yellow flecks beneath rafters, and a bitter metallic odor when the air turns warm. Then the shells split. Historical barns became death traps when newly emerged Beedrill dropped downward before their wings dried, crawling across walls, tools, animals, and people in panic.

Kakuna are also tied to punishment folklore. Criminals were once tied to trees in Weedle woods overnight as a trial of innocence. If they survived, the forest had judged them clean. If Kakuna appeared in the tree above them, the sentence was considered delayed but certain. In truth, humans were using the forest to avoid admitting they were cruel.

Kakuna handling rules are written more like explosive safety than animal care: do not shake the branch, heat the cluster, store Kakuna indoors unless emergence containment is ready, cut down a Kakuna tree in warm weather, crack the shell to check progress, or battle-train a Kakuna repeatedly for amusement. If one clicks, step back. If several click, leave. If the whole tree clicks, do not run under the branches. If you find empty shells hanging open and wet, do not stand admiring them. The Beedrill are close enough to still remember the tree.

sections.title: Origin Story
sections.content:
Kakuna was confirmed in 1800 when marked Weedle climbed, fastened themselves, and hardened into golden pupal forms.

Carrow wrote: not dead, not larval, armed in preparation. Villages learned that a Weedle swarm was only the beginning of a delayed Beedrill event.

Kakuna was formally confirmed in 1800, two years after Weedle was recorded during the Bramble Wake Incident. Hardened yellow forms had been known long before that under names like thorn pods, priest candles, hanging teeth, and soldier seeds. Some thought they were plant galls. Others believed they were dead Weedle wrapped in venom. Local knowledge already suspected the truth, but science required marked proof.

After 1798, Iven Carrow marked several live Weedle with mineral stains and released them into a contained bramble enclosure. For days they fed, raised horns at shadows, and struck anything disturbing the leaves. Then they climbed into the inner hedge, fastened themselves with silk-like anchors, and stiffened. Their striped bodies tightened. The outer skin dulled and hardened. The horn withdrew into the changing structure. By the third day, the enclosure held Kakuna.

Carrow’s first confirmed sketch from 1800 showed three marked specimens hanging from a bramble arch, each angled outward. Beneath it he wrote: “Not dead. Not larval. Armed in preparation.” The confirmation changed how villages understood Weedle infestations. Weedle were not the final threat. If larvae fed and hardened in large numbers, the true danger came later, when Beedrill emerged in the same area.

Seasonal management began after 1800: map Weedle feeding zones, inspect hedges before hardening season, relocate clusters only with trained handlers, and avoid burning except under cold wet conditions with containment ready. Villagers learned that live Kakuna are heavier than they look and faintly warm in the hand.

The Yellow Wake of 1801 became the most notorious early event. A grain merchant stored twenty-three live Kakuna in warehouse rafters after hearing they repelled mice. For several days, no rodents entered. Then warm weather arrived early and the Kakuna emerged overnight. Three workers were trapped in the loft. Two survived under flour sacks. The third reached the ladder while newly emerged Beedrill crawled over the beams. His body was recovered near the door, hand still on the latch.

Kakuna became a symbol of delayed consequence. Some graveyards installed iron hooks to remove clusters before burial days. Others left distant clusters untouched, believing they guarded the dead from scavengers. There was practical truth beneath superstition: Beedrill did drive away scavengers. They also drove away mourners. By 1805, watched Kakuna clusters split open and produced the spear-armed adults long feared in Kanto forests. Weedle was the sting in the grass. Kakuna was the sealed chamber. Beedrill was the punishment for thinking stillness meant safety.

sections.title: Field Rules Background
items: Do not crack shells to check progress.

sections.title: Cultural Notes
items: Kakuna near graves meant delayed consequence.
